Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I tried to set aside time in the evenings to go through materials, but with 18 online courses, I felt completely overwhelmed and scattered. I'm a visual learner, so I would try to create diagrams and charts from the content, but I rarely had a consistent schedule. Most weeks I'd study maybe 19 hours total, but it was spread chaotically across different days whenever I could find the energy.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how scared I was feeling about falling behind. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, then panic would set in and I'd try to cram everything at once. Deadlines definitely drove when I studied more than any personal routine I tried to establish. The stress of juggling so many courses meant I was always reacting rather than planning ahead.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in, if I'm being honest. I'd get this anxious feeling that I was missing something important or falling behind, and I'd quickly check what was due. Sometimes I logged in just to see if there were announcements or if I'd missed a deadline. It wasn't really about excitement to learn—it was more about damage control.
Q4: I wouldn't say it ever became a comfortable routine for me. There were brief periods where I'd check in daily for a week or two, but then life would get overwhelming and I'd fall off. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day—it always felt like this thing I had to force myself to face. I wish I could say it became automatic, but the anxiety around it made that impossible.
Q5: The video content was most useful for me because I learn best when I can see things visually explained. I watched over 160 minutes of videos, and those helped concepts click in ways that reading alone couldn't. I also found the discussion posts helpful when other students shared their perspectives—it made me feel less alone in struggling with the material. Visual diagrams and any content with graphics really stuck with me better.
Q6: I often ignored the lengthy reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. With 18 courses, I simply couldn't engage with everything, so I had to triage. The resources that were text-heavy without visual elements felt like a wall I couldn't climb. I also delayed discussion participation because putting my thoughts out there felt vulnerable when I was already feeling so behind and scared.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand something I was struggling with, I'd engage with it. Visual content that broke down complex ideas made me feel like my time was well spent. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades got pushed aside because I simply didn't have the bandwidth.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. With required work already overwhelming me, optional content felt like extra weight I couldn't carry. Required activities had this urgency attached—I had to do them or face consequences. Optional ones, even if interesting, just made me feel guilty because I knew I should engage but couldn't find the energy or time.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and would study them on my tablet without being connected to the internet. I made handwritten notes with colored pens and created visual mind maps that helped me process information better. I also used YouTube videos to supplement concepts I didn't understand from the course materials. A lot of my real learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be at my desk with printed materials and my notebook, working through problems and creating diagrams, but none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes before exams. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over the phone, working through ideas together. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study effort.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it offline for days. My internet isn't always reliable, so I learned to grab materials when I could and study them later. I'd watch the same downloaded video multiple times while making notes, but the system would only show that single initial access. This was actually my primary way of engaging with visual content.
Q12: There were several points where everything felt impossible. When multiple deadlines from different courses collided, I'd freeze up completely. My stress levels were through the roof, and I felt scared about failing. The sheer volume of 18 courses meant there was always something demanding attention, and sometimes I just shut down and couldn't face any of it. Those were dark periods where logging in felt terrifying.
Q13: Absolutely, all of those factors affected me constantly. The workload was genuinely unmanageable with so many courses. When I was confused about material, I'd avoid the LMS because facing it made me feel stupid. Fatigue was constant—I was exhausted from trying to keep up. Some weeks I had family responsibilities that took priority, and the courses had to wait even though it scared me.
Q14: Many times I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. I'd scroll through content while my mind was elsewhere, worried about other deadlines or just too tired to focus. Sometimes I'd have a video playing but I wasn't actually watching—I was just going through the motions. It looked like engagement but it was really just presence without processing.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they mattered. The fear of not doing them well enough made me avoid starting them. With my assignment completion at about 64%, you can see how much I struggled with this. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more prepared, but tomorrow I'd feel just as scared and unprepared. It was a vicious cycle of avoidance.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my only motivator. I'd ignore things until a deadline loomed, then panic and try to do everything at once. The LMS became this place I'd frantically visit right before things were due. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. But the deadline-driven approach meant I never really learned deeply—I was just surviving.
Q17: Feedback was complicated for me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me briefly, but negative feedback made me want to hide. With my exam scores being so low, seeing those results made me scared to engage further. I'd sometimes avoid checking grades because I couldn't handle the disappointment. The CBT sessions I had helped me process some of this, but the fear around feedback remained strong.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with so much content spread across the platform. Having 18 courses meant I could never fully commit to any single course's structure. I appreciated when modules were clearly organized with visual elements, but often I felt lost in the maze of materials. The structure assumed students could dedicate significant time to each course, which wasn't my reality.
Q19: Clear visual organization made engagement easier—when I could see at a glance what needed to be done, I felt less overwhelmed. What made it harder was when content was buried in multiple clicks or when instructions were unclear. The lack of visual progress indicators stressed me out because I couldn't easily see how behind I was. Having so many courses also meant the design of each one competed for my limited attention.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I logged in 25 times and watched videos and checked things, but my actual submissions were incomplete. Sometimes you're searching for understanding, trying to figure out what's expected, or just anxiously checking without producing work. High activity can mean struggling and searching rather than confidently completing. Fear can drive you to check constantly without actually submitting anything.
Q21: That could reflect someone who studies effectively offline, which I tried to do. If someone downloads materials and studies deeply away from the platform, their LMS activity would look minimal but their learning could be solid. Unfortunately for me, even my offline studying didn't translate to good exam performance, so I can't claim this describes my success. But the concept makes sense—visible activity isn't the same as learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you access things and for how long, which reveals some behavioral trends. But they completely miss the quality of engagement—whether you're actually thinking or just scrolling. They miss offline study, emotional state, and the reasons behind the numbers. My logs would show low engagement, but they wouldn't show the hours I spent with printed notes or how scared I was every time I logged in.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It means thinking about concepts beyond what's required, asking questions, and connecting ideas. For me, true engagement would feel like curiosity rather than fear. This semester, I was rarely engaged in that meaningful way—I was mostly just trying to survive and not fail completely, which isn't the same thing at all.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how scared I felt every single day about failing. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with handwritten notes and visual diagrams I created. It wouldn't show the mental health struggles or the CBT sessions I needed to cope. It also wouldn't reveal that I was drowning in 18 courses with medium resources and no real support system. The numbers tell a story, but not my whole story.
